section.title: Gethsemane, Arrest, and Peter's Denial
section.sectionRationale: The night of anguish and abandonment. In Gethsemane Jesus wrestles in prayer with the horror of the cup He must drink, surrendering His will to the Father while His disciples sleep. He is betrayed with a kiss, arrested, and deserted by all. Before the Sanhedrin He confesses openly that He is the Christ, the Son of Man who will come in glory, and is condemned. Meanwhile Peter, in the courtyard, denies Him three times and weeps bitterly.
section.thesis: Facing the cup of God's judgment, Jesus submits His will to the Father and goes willingly to arrest, false trial, and condemnation, confessing His true identity even as He is betrayed, deserted, and denied, so that the sinless Son bears the collapse of every human loyalty around Him.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the agony in Gethsemane; the betrayal and arrest; the trial before the Sanhedrin and the good confession; and Peter's threefold denial.

units.label: The agony in Gethsemane
units.note: not what I will, but what You will

verses.text: Then they came to a place called Gethsemane, and Jesus told His disciples, “Sit here while I pray.”
He took with Him Peter, James, and John, and began to be deeply troubled and distressed.
Then He said to them, “My soul is consumed with sorrow to the point of death. Stay here and keep {{gregoreo|watch}}.”
Going a little farther, He fell to the ground and prayed that, if it were possible, the hour would pass from Him.
“{{abba|Abba}}, Father,” He said, “all things are possible for You. Take this {{poterion|cup}} from Me. Yet not what I will, but what You will.”
Then Jesus returned and found them sleeping. “Simon, are you asleep?” He asked. “Were you not able to keep watch for one hour?
Watch and pray so that you will not enter into temptation. For the spirit is willing, but the body is weak.”
Again He went away and prayed, saying the same thing.
And again Jesus returned and found them sleeping, for their eyes were heavy. And they did not know what to answer Him.
When Jesus returned the third time, He said, “Are you still sleeping and resting? That is enough! The hour has come. Look, the Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners.
Rise, let us go. See, My betrayer is approaching!”

units.label: The betrayal and arrest
units.note: the one I kiss is the man

verses.text: While Jesus was still speaking, Judas, one of the Twelve, arrived, accompanied by a crowd armed with swords and clubs, sent from the chief priests, scribes, and elders.
Now the betrayer had arranged a signal with them: “The One I kiss is the man; arrest Him and lead Him away securely.”
Going directly to Jesus, he said, “Rabbi!” and kissed Him.
Then the men seized Jesus and arrested Him.
And one of the bystanders drew his sword and struck the servant of the high priest, cutting off his ear.
Jesus asked the crowd, “Have you come out with swords and clubs to arrest Me as you would an outlaw?
Every day I was with you, teaching in the temple courts, and you did not arrest Me. But this has happened that the Scriptures would be fulfilled.”
Then everyone deserted Him and fled.
One young man who had been following Jesus was wearing a linen cloth around his body. They caught hold of him,
but he pulled free of the linen cloth and ran away naked.

units.label: The trial and the good confession
units.note: I am, and you will see the Son of Man

verses.text: They led Jesus away to the high priest, and all the chief priests, elders, and scribes assembled.
Peter followed Him at a distance, right into the courtyard of the high priest. And he sat with the officers and warmed himself by the fire.
Now the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin were seeking testimony against Jesus to put Him to death, but they did not find any.
For many bore false witness against Jesus, but their testimony was inconsistent.
Then some men stood up and testified falsely against Him:
“We heard Him say, ‘I will destroy this man-made temple, and in three days I will build another that is made without hands.’”
But even their testimony was inconsistent.
So the high priest stood up before them and questioned Jesus, “Have You no answer? What are these men testifying against You?”
But Jesus remained silent and made no reply. Again the high priest questioned Him, “Are You the Christ, the Son of the Blessed One?”
“{{egoeimi|I am}},” said Jesus, “and you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of Power and coming with the clouds of heaven.”
At this, the high priest tore his clothes and declared, “Why do we need any more witnesses?
You have heard the blasphemy. What is your verdict?” And they all condemned Him as deserving of death.
Then some of them began to spit on Him. They blindfolded Him, struck Him with their fists, and said to Him, “Prophesy!” And the officers received Him with slaps in His face.
While Peter was in the courtyard below, one of the servant girls of the high priest came down
and saw him warming himself there. She looked at Peter and said, “You also were with Jesus the Nazarene.”
But he denied it. “I do not know or even understand what you are talking about,” he said. Then he went out to the gateway, and the rooster crowed.
There the servant girl saw him and again said to those standing nearby, “This man is one of them.”
But he denied it again. After a little while, those standing nearby said once more to Peter, “Surely you are one of them, for you too are a Galilean.”
But he began to curse and swear, “I do not know this man of whom you speak!”

And immediately the rooster crowed a second time. Then Peter remembered the word that Jesus had spoken to him: “Before the rooster crows twice, you will deny Me three times.” And he broke down and wept.

gregoreo.gloss: To stay awake, be alert, keep watch. Jesus asks the three to watch with Him and to watch and pray against temptation, but they sleep. The failure to keep watch in the garden foreshadows their collapse when the hour of trial comes, a warning He had pressed in the Olivet Discourse.

abba.gloss: Father, the intimate Aramaic word a child uses. Jesus prays Abba, Father, in the deepest anguish, showing that even at the edge of the cross He rests in the Father's love. Believers, adopted in Christ, are given the same cry by the Spirit, Romans 8:15.

poterion.gloss: A cup. In the prophets the cup is the cup of God's wrath and judgment. The cup Jesus shrinks from yet accepts is the cup of divine judgment against sin, which He will drink to the dregs in His people's place at the cross.

egoeimi.gloss: I am. Asked directly whether He is the Christ, the Son of the Blessed One, Jesus answers plainly, I am, and adds the vision of the Son of Man at God's right hand. It is both a claim to be the Messiah and, echoing the divine name and Daniel 7, a claim to divine glory.

basic.context: In the garden of Gethsemane, Jesus is overwhelmed with sorrow. He falls to the ground and prays that, if possible, the cup might pass, yet surrenders: not what I will, but what You will. Three times He returns to find Peter, James, and John asleep. Judas arrives with an armed crowd and betrays Jesus with a kiss; Jesus is arrested, a bystander cuts off a servant's ear, and all the disciples flee, one even leaving his garment to escape naked. Jesus is taken to the high priest, where false witnesses fail to agree. When directly asked if He is the Christ, He answers, I am, and speaks of the Son of Man coming in glory. The council condemns Him for blasphemy and abuses Him. Outside, Peter denies knowing Jesus three times, then hears the rooster, remembers Jesus' words, and weeps.
basic.hermeneutics: Gethsemane shows the real cost of the cross: not merely physical pain but the cup of God's judgment, which makes even the sinless Son recoil, yet He submits. The scene of desertion, betrayal, and denial surrounds Jesus with human failure, so that His faithfulness stands alone. His confession before the Sanhedrin is the clear, public claim to be the Christ and the divine Son of Man, the very truth for which He is condemned.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus asks the three to gregoreo, keep watch, but they sleep. He prays abba, Father, that the poterion, the cup, might pass, yet yields to the Father's will. Before the council He confesses egoeimi, I am, the Christ and Son of Man.
basic.typology: The cup Jesus drinks is the cup of God's wrath in the prophets. The struck Shepherd is deserted by the scattered sheep, fulfilling Zechariah. The silent, falsely accused sufferer fulfils the servant of Isaiah 53.
basic.christ: Jesus is the obedient Son who drinks the cup of judgment, the Christ and the Son of Man of Daniel 7, and the faithful witness who confesses the truth even unto death. He stands firm where all others fail.
basic.god: The Father's will is that the Son drink the cup for sinners, and the Son submits in trusting love. God's plan is fulfilled precisely, even through betrayal, false trial, and denial.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 51 and Psalm 75 speak of the cup of wrath. Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man. Isaiah 53 foretells the silent, condemned servant.
basic.oneStory: In the garden the second Adam does what the first did not: He obeys God's will even at the cost of His life, taking up the cup of judgment we deserved. Betrayed, deserted, condemned, and denied, He stands alone as the faithful one, moving toward the cross where He will drink the cup to the dregs so that His failing people may be forgiven and restored, as Peter's tears already hint.

context.literary: This section continues Mark's Passion narrative from the Last Supper. Gethsemane answers the confident boasts of the disciples in the previous scene, exposing their weakness as they sleep. Mark intertwines Jesus' faithful confession before the Sanhedrin, verses 53 to 65, with Peter's denial in the courtyard, verses 66 to 72, so the two are read together: the Master confesses while the disciple denies.
context.historical: Gethsemane was an olive grove on the Mount of Olives. A night arrest by an armed party avoided the crowds. The Sanhedrin was the Jewish ruling council; a capital trial at night with paid false witnesses violated proper procedure. Tearing one's clothes signified horror at blasphemy. Charges of threatening the temple and of blasphemy were the gravest, and a Galilean accent gave Peter away in Jerusalem.
context.canonical: The cup recalls the cup of God's wrath, Isaiah 51:17 and Psalm 75:8, which Jesus drinks in His people's place. His answer to the high priest fuses Psalm 110:1, sitting at God's right hand, with Daniel 7:13, coming with the clouds, an unmistakable claim to messianic and divine authority. His silence before false accusers fulfils Isaiah 53:7, and the desertion fulfils Zechariah 13:7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the true horror of the cross as the cup of God's judgment, to display Jesus' obedient submission to the Father amid betrayal and desertion, to record His unambiguous confession of His identity as Christ and Son of Man, and to show, through Peter, both the depth of human failure and the seed of restoration in his tears.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the unrepeatable events of Jesus' Passion. Prescriptively, Jesus' prayer models submission to God's will, and His command to watch and pray against temptation applies to all disciples. Peter's denial warns against self-confidence, and his weeping points to the repentance that leads to restoration.

debates.question: What is the cup Jesus prays to have removed?

views.label: The cup of God's judgment against sin
views.case: Throughout the prophets the cup is divine wrath; Jesus recoils not merely from death but from bearing the full weight of God's judgment on sin in His people's place, the deepest agony of the cross.

views.label: The whole ordeal of the Passion
views.case: The cup embraces the suffering, shame, and death that lie ahead; Jesus, truly human, shrinks from the horror of it all, yet embraces the Father's will, showing genuine anguish and genuine obedience.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the cup is dreadful precisely because it involves bearing judgment for sin, and that Jesus, in real human anguish, freely submits to drink it for us.

debates.question: Why does Jesus answer the high priest so openly when He was often reticent before?

views.label: The hour of revelation has come
views.case: The reticence, the so-called messianic secret, protected His mission until the cross; now, at the decisive hour, He confesses plainly, since His true kingship is about to be revealed in His death and vindication.

views.label: A deliberate, sworn confession before the council
views.case: Put under solemn questioning, Jesus gives the good confession, affirming He is the Christ and the divine Son of Man of Daniel 7, sealing His own condemnation and bearing witness to the truth before His judges.

debates.bearing: Both hold that Jesus now openly declares who He is, at the very moment it will cost Him His life, so that His identity is publicly confessed on the way to the cross.

study.originalLanguages: In the garden Jesus asks the three to gregoreo, keep watch, but they sleep, foreshadowing their coming collapse. He prays abba, Father, the intimate cry of a trusting child, that the poterion, the cup, might pass, the cup of God's judgment the prophets describe, yet He yields: not what I will, but what You will. Hours later, under oath before the high priest, He answers the question whether He is the Christ with egoeimi, I am, and joins Psalm 110 and Daniel 7 to claim the glory of the Son of Man at God's right hand. The obedient submission of the garden and the fearless confession of the courtroom together show the faithful Son moving willingly toward the cross.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus drinking the cup of divine judgment in His people's place, Isaiah 51:17 and Psalm 75:8.

typology.type: The struck Shepherd deserted by the sheep
typology.fulfillment: The disciples fleeing as Jesus is arrested, fulfilling Zechariah 13:7 and Mark 14:27.
Jesus falsely accused and led to death without opening His mouth in His own defense, Isaiah 53:7.

study.christ: Here the sinless Son faces the full horror of what lies ahead, the cup of God's judgment, and in genuine anguish He surrenders His will to the Father: not what I will, but what You will. He is the second Adam who obeys in a garden where the first rebelled. Betrayed by a friend's kiss, abandoned by every disciple, tried on false charges, and abused by His judges, He stands utterly alone as the faithful one. Yet He speaks the good confession, I am the Christ, the Son of Man who will come in glory, sealing His death by telling the truth. He is the obedient, faithful, willing Saviour, drinking the cup so His failing people need not.
study.god: The Father's will is the axis of the whole scene: it is His will that the Son drink the cup of judgment for sinners, and the Son embraces that will in trusting love. God's saving plan is accomplished with exact fidelity to Scripture, even through the betrayal of Judas, the desertion of the disciples, the injustice of a rigged trial, and the denial of Peter. Nothing spins out of God's hands; the very failures of men become the path by which the Son is condemned in our place. This is a God who saves through the obedient suffering of His Son and who, as Peter's tears foreshadow, will restore even those who fail.

crossReferences.note: The cup of the LORD's wrath, drained to the dregs, the cup Jesus takes up in Gethsemane.
The Son of Man coming with the clouds and given dominion, which Jesus claims before the Sanhedrin.
Like a lamb led to slaughter, He did not open His mouth, fulfilled in Jesus' silence before false witnesses.
By the Spirit we cry Abba, Father, the very cry Jesus prays, now given to His adopted brothers and sisters.

study.oneStory: In a garden the first Adam grasped for his own will; in Gethsemane the second Adam surrenders His, taking up the cup of judgment we deserved with the words, not what I will, but what You will. Betrayed, deserted, falsely tried, and denied, Jesus stands alone as the faithful one, confessing openly that He is the Christ and the Son of Man even as it seals His death. All human loyalty collapses around Him, yet He does not waver. He moves toward the cross to drink the cup to its dregs, so that failing sinners, even a Peter weeping in the courtyard, may be forgiven and restored.

quiz.q: What did Jesus pray in Gethsemane?

options.text: Abba, Father, take this cup from Me, yet not what I will, but what You will.
options.note: Mark 14:36; genuine anguish joined to full submission to the Father's will.

options.text: That He might avoid the cross entirely.
options.note: He asked if the cup could pass, but decisively submitted to the Father's will, not seeking to avoid it.

options.text: That the Father's will be done even at the cost of the cup.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should include His honest plea for the cup to pass if possible.

options.text: That His disciples would be spared any suffering.
options.note: His prayer concerned the cup He must drink and submission to the Father, not sparing the disciples.

quiz.q: What is the cup Jesus shrinks from yet accepts?

options.text: The cup of God's judgment against sin, which He will drink in His people's place.
options.note: In the prophets the cup is divine wrath; Jesus bears it for sinners at the cross.

options.text: Simply the physical pain of crucifixion.
options.note: The physical pain is real, but the cup's dread is bearing God's judgment on sin, far deeper than pain alone.

options.text: The whole ordeal of suffering, shame, and death He must endure.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but its horror lies specifically in bearing God's judgment for sin.

options.text: A test of His courage with no deeper meaning.
options.note: The cup is the cup of God's wrath, not a mere test of nerve.

quiz.q: How did the disciples respond when Jesus was arrested?

options.text: They all deserted Him and fled, one even leaving his garment to escape.
options.note: Mark 14:50-52; the sheep scattered, fulfilling Jesus' word from Zechariah.

options.text: They fought bravely to defend Him.
options.note: One struck with a sword, but then all fled; they did not defend Him.

options.text: They abandoned Him and ran away in fear.
options.note: Correct, but it should note the vivid detail of even the young man fleeing naked, underscoring the total desertion.

options.text: They followed the soldiers quietly to the high priest.
options.note: Only Peter followed at a distance; the rest fled outright.

quiz.q: How did Jesus answer the high priest's question whether He was the Christ?

options.text: I am, and you will see the Son of Man seated at God's right hand and coming with the clouds.
options.note: Mark 14:62; a clear claim to be the Messiah and the divine Son of Man.

options.text: He remained silent and gave no answer.
options.note: He was silent to the false witnesses, but to the direct question He answered plainly, I am.

options.text: He affirmed He was the Christ and the glorious Son of Man.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should include the vivid words about the right hand of Power and the clouds.

options.text: He denied being the Christ to avoid condemnation.
options.note: He openly confessed it, which led to His condemnation for blasphemy.

quiz.q: How did Peter respond when questioned in the courtyard, and what followed?

options.text: He denied Jesus three times, then heard the rooster, remembered Jesus' words, and wept bitterly.
options.note: Mark 14:66-72; denial, then remembrance and tears of repentance.

options.text: He denied Jesus but felt no remorse afterward.
options.note: The opposite; he broke down and wept, a sign of genuine grief and coming restoration.

options.text: He denied Jesus three times and then repented in sorrow.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should note the rooster and his remembering Jesus' specific word.

options.text: He boldly confessed Jesus before the servants.
options.note: He had vowed to, but in fact he denied Him three times.

questions: In deep anguish Jesus still prayed, not what I will, but what You will. Where is God calling you to surrender your will to His, even when it is costly?
The disciples slept when Jesus asked them to watch and pray. What helps or hinders your own watchfulness and prayer in seasons of testing?
Jesus was betrayed, deserted, and denied, yet remained faithful. How does His faithfulness in the face of total abandonment strengthen you?
Jesus confessed the truth about Himself knowing it would cost His life. Where are you tempted to stay silent about Christ to protect yourself?
Peter denied Jesus, then wept, and would later be restored. How does Peter's failure and grief give you hope when you have failed the Lord?

translationNotes.note: Abba is the intimate Aramaic word for Father; Mark preserves Jesus' actual cry. The same cry is given to believers by the Spirit of adoption, Romans 8:15 and Galatians 4:6.

I am answers the high priest directly; the added words draw on Psalm 110:1 and Daniel 7:13, claiming both messianic kingship and the divine glory of the Son of Man, which the council judged blasphemy.
Some manuscripts omit and the rooster crowed here; either way Jesus' prediction of a denial before the rooster crows twice is precisely fulfilled in verse 72.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 14:32-72. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
